questions.prompt:
Read: "We're almost ready to cook. We have a lot of vegetables and a few eggs, but there isn't much oil and we don't have any rice."
Which sentence is TRUE?

options.text: There is plenty of oil.
They have no rice.
They have a lot of eggs.
They have only a little vegetables.

questions.explanation: "We don't have any rice" means they have no rice, so (b) is true. (a) is false — there isn't much oil; (c) overstates it — only a few eggs; and (d) is wrong twice — they have a lot of vegetables, and vegetables (countable) would take a few, not a little.

options.text: How many pieces of furniture are there?
There isn't much furniture in the room.
We bought some new furnitures.
There is a lot of furniture here.

questions.explanation: The error is (c): furniture is uncountable, so it has no plural — never "furnitures". To count it we say pieces of furniture (a). (b) and (d) correctly use much and a lot of with the uncountable noun.

questions.prompt: Complete: "There were ___ people at the concert, but ___ of them stayed until the end."

options.text: much / a little
many / few
a little / many
a great deal / a few

questions.explanation: People is countable and plural, so both gaps need countable quantifiers: many people, and few of them (meaning "not many"). So it is many / few. Much, a little and a great deal are for uncountable nouns and don't fit people.

options.text: I don't have some money, so I can't buy many bread.
I don't have many money, so I can't buy any breads.
I haven't any money, so I can't buy a few bread.
I don't have any money, so I can't buy much bread.

questions.explanation: In negatives we use any (any money), and bread is uncountable, so we measure it with much (much bread): that is (d). (a) wrongly uses some in a negative and many with bread; (b) uses many with the uncountable money and pluralizes bread; (c) uses a few with uncountable bread.

questions.prompt: Complete: "There is ___ traffic this morning, so the bus will be late."

options.text: a lot of
many
a few
a great number of

questions.explanation: Traffic is uncountable, and a lot of works with uncountable nouns: a lot of traffic. Many, a few and a great number of all require countable plural nouns, which traffic is not (we never say "traffics").

questions.prompt:
Read: "Sami had very little money left. He counted his coins: he had just enough for one ticket, but not for a drink as well."
Which statement is TRUE?

options.text: Sami had a lot of money.
Sami could buy a ticket and a drink.
Sami had almost no money left.
Sami had many money.

questions.explanation: "Very little money" means almost none, so (c) is true. (a) says the opposite; (b) contradicts "not for a drink as well"; and (d) is ungrammatical anyway — money is uncountable, so it takes much/little, never "many money".
